seo.imageAlt: A bowl of authentic Congee with Century Egg garnished with green onions and ginger, perfect for a Chinese breakfast.
seo.imageCaption: Savor the rich, comforting flavors of homemade Congee with Century Egg – a classic Chinese breakfast.

description: Congee with Century Egg is a beloved Chinese comfort food, a savory rice porridge perfect for a heartwarming breakfast or a light, nourishing meal. Its creamy texture combined with the distinctive, umami-rich flavor of century egg creates a truly satisfying and unique culinary experience. Often garnished with fresh ginger and vibrant green onions, this classic dish offers a delicate balance of flavors and a wonderfully soothing consistency.

ingredients.notes: short grain or medium grain also works
optional, for marinating pork or serving

instructions.title: Prepare the Rice
instructions.text: Rinse the jasmine rice thoroughly under cold running water until the water runs clear. For a creamier congee, you can soak the rice in 2 cups of water for at least 30 minutes, or even overnight. Drain well before cooking.

instructions.title: Start the Congee Base
instructions.text: In a large pot, combine the rinsed rice with 8-10 cups of fresh water. Bring it to a rolling boil over high heat, then immediately reduce the heat to low. Cover the pot, leaving a small gap for steam to escape, and simmer gently.

instructions.title: Prepare Century Eggs and Optional Pork
instructions.text: While the congee simmers, peel the century eggs and dice them into small, bite-sized pieces. If using pork mince, marinate it with a dash of soy sauce and white pepper. Finely julienne the fresh ginger and chop the green onions, reserving some for garnish.

instructions.title: Cook the Congee to Desired Consistency
instructions.text: Continue to simmer the congee for 30-45 minutes, stirring occasionally to prevent sticking and ensure even cooking. The rice grains should break down and the porridge should thicken to a creamy consistency. Add more hot water if the congee becomes too thick.

instructions.title: Add Century Eggs and Pork
instructions.text: Once the congee reaches your desired consistency, stir in the diced century eggs and the marinated pork mince (if using). If adding pork, ensure it is cooked through, usually within 3-5 minutes. Stir in most of the julienned ginger.

instructions.title: Season and Serve
instructions.text: Season the congee with salt and a pinch of white pepper to taste. Ladle the hot congee into individual bowls. Drizzle with a few drops of sesame oil and garnish generously with the remaining fresh julienned ginger and chopped green onions. Serve immediately.

tips: For extra creamy congee, freeze the rinsed rice for a few hours or overnight before cooking. The freezing process breaks down the rice grains, allowing them to soften and disintegrate faster when boiled.
Do not add salt or soy sauce to the congee until the very end of cooking. Adding salt too early can toughen the rice grains and prevent them from breaking down properly.
Congee can be stored in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 3 days. When reheating, add a splash of water or broth to loosen it up, as it will thicken considerably when cold.
article:
Congee with Century Egg: A Timeless Chinese Breakfast Delight

In the vast and varied tapestry of Chinese cuisine, few dishes evoke as much comfort and nostalgia as a warm bowl of congee. This humble rice porridge, often served savory, holds a special place in the hearts of many, particularly as a nourishing breakfast. Among its myriad variations, Congee with Century Egg stands out as a classic, offering a unique blend of creamy texture, savory depth, and an intriguing umami kick. It's a dish that embodies both simplicity and sophistication, a perfect start to any day, or a soothing meal when you need a gentle pick-me-up. If you've ever yearned to recreate the authentic flavors of a traditional Chinese breakfast, mastering Congee with Century Egg is an excellent place to begin your culinary journey.

## What is Congee with Century Egg?

At its core, congee is a rice porridge, typically made by boiling rice in a large amount of water or broth until the grains break down into a thick, gruel-like consistency. It can range from thin and soupy to thick and creamy, depending on the desired texture and cooking time. Congee serves as a versatile canvas, absorbing the flavors of whatever ingredients are added to it. It's a staple across Asia, known for its digestibility and comforting properties, often served to the sick or young children.

The star ingredient that elevates this particular congee is the century egg, also known as pidan or preserved egg. Despite its name, it’s not aged for a hundred years, but rather preserved for several weeks or months in a mixture of clay, ash, salt, quicklime, and rice hulls. This unique curing process transforms a regular duck, chicken, or quail egg into a culinary marvel. The egg white becomes a translucent, dark brown, jelly-like substance, while the yolk turns a dark green to gray color with a creamy texture and a distinct, pungent aroma. The flavor profile of a century egg is complex – earthy, intensely savory, slightly sulfurous, and wonderfully umami-rich. When combined with the mild, comforting backdrop of congee, the century egg provides a delightful contrast in both texture and taste, making Congee with Century Egg an unforgettable experience.

## Ingredients You Need

Crafting a delicious bowl of Congee with Century Egg requires a few key ingredients, most of which are readily available. The beauty of congee lies in its flexibility, so feel free to adjust quantities to your preference.

*   **Rice:** 1 cup Jasmine rice or short-grain rice. Short-grain rice tends to yield a creamier congee.
*   **Water or Broth:** 8-10 cups water or chicken/pork broth. Broth will add significantly more flavor.
*   **Century Eggs:** 2-3 century eggs, peeled and cut into small wedges or cubes.
*   **Pork (optional but recommended):** 4-6 oz lean pork shoulder, tenderloin, or ground pork, thinly sliced or minced.
*   **Ginger:** 1-inch piece, peeled and julienned or thinly sliced.
*   **Green Onions (Scallions):** 2-3 stalks, chopped, for garnish.
*   **Salt:** To taste.
*   **White Pepper:** To taste.
*   **Sesame Oil:** A drizzle for serving.
*   **Optional for pork marinade:** 1 tsp Shaoxing wine, 1 tsp cornstarch, 1/2 tsp soy sauce.

## Step-by-Step Instructions

Making Congee with Century Egg is a journey of patience, but the reward is a deeply satisfying meal.

1.  **Prepare the Rice:** Rinse the rice thoroughly under cold water until the water runs clear. This removes excess starch, preventing the congee from becoming too gummy. For an even creamier and faster-cooking congee, you can soak the rice in water for at least 30 minutes, or even freeze it overnight (thaw before cooking).
2.  **Prepare the Pork (if using):** If using pork, thinly slice or mince it. Marinate with Shaoxing wine, cornstarch, and soy sauce for 15-20 minutes. This tenderizes the pork and adds flavor.
3.  **Prepare Century Eggs and Ginger:** Carefully peel the century eggs. They can be a bit delicate. Once peeled, cut them into small wedges or cubes. Julienne or thinly slice the fresh ginger.
4.  **Start the Congee:** In a large, heavy-bottomed pot or Dutch oven, combine the rinsed rice and 8-10 cups of water or broth. Bring the mixture to a rolling boil over high heat.
5.  **Simmer and Stir:** Once boiling, reduce the heat to very low, cover the pot partially (leaving a small gap for steam to escape), and let it simmer. Stir the congee every 15-20 minutes to prevent the rice from sticking to the bottom and burning. This also helps break down the rice grains, creating that signature creamy texture. Cook for 1 to 1.5 hours, or until the rice grains have fully broken down and the congee has reached your desired consistency. If it becomes too thick, add a little more hot water or broth.
6.  **Add Pork and Ginger:** About 30 minutes before the congee is done, add the marinated pork and most of the julienned ginger to the pot. Stir well to ensure the pork separates and cooks evenly.
7.  **Add Century Eggs:** Towards the end of cooking (about 5-10 minutes before serving), stir in the cut century eggs. Some prefer to add them as a garnish at the very end to retain their distinct texture and appearance.
8.  **Season:** Season the Congee with Century Egg generously with salt and white pepper to taste. Remember that broths can vary in saltiness, so taste before adding too much.
9.  **Serve:** Ladle the hot congee into individual bowls. Garnish with chopped green onions, a drizzle of sesame oil, and any remaining julienned ginger.

## Pro Tips for the Best Results

*   **Rice Quality:** Use good quality Jasmine or short-grain rice. The starch content contributes significantly to the creaminess.
*   **Freezing Rice:** A fantastic hack for speeding up congee cooking and achieving an incredibly creamy texture is to freeze your rinsed rice overnight. The freezing and thawing process breaks down the starch cells, allowing them to release more easily during cooking.
*   **Broth is King:** While water works, using chicken or pork broth will elevate the flavor profile of your Congee with Century Egg immensely. Consider making your own broth for the best results.
*   **Low and Slow:** Patience is key. Congee benefits from a long, gentle simmer. Don't rush it, as this is how the rice breaks down to create that wonderful, velvety texture.
*   **Stirring Frequency:** Stirring prevents sticking and helps develop the texture, but don't overdo it. Too much stirring can make the congee gluey.
*   **Consistency Control:** If your congee is too thick, add more hot water or broth. If it's too thin, continue simmering uncovered until it thickens.
*   **Century Egg Integration:** Adding century eggs during the last few minutes of cooking allows their unique flavor to meld into the congee while retaining some of their texture. If you prefer a stronger, more distinct century egg flavor and appearance, add them as a garnish just before serving.

## Serving Suggestions

Congee with Century Egg is best enjoyed hot, immediately after cooking. It’s a complete meal in itself, but its flavors are often enhanced by a few simple garnishes and accompaniments.

*   **Garnishes:** Always have a generous sprinkle of fresh chopped green onions (scallions), a dash of white pepper, and a drizzle of fragrant sesame oil. Some also enjoy a scattering of fresh cilantro or crispy fried shallots for added texture and aroma.
*   **Accompaniments:** For a truly authentic Chinese breakfast experience, serve your Congee with Century Egg alongside freshly fried *youtiao* (Chinese crullers or fried dough sticks) for dipping. Pickled vegetables, like pickled radish or mustard greens, offer a refreshing, tangy contrast. A dash of light soy sauce can also be added for extra savory depth.

## Variations and Substitutions

While Congee with Century Egg is a classic, the beauty of congee lies in its adaptability.

*   **Meat Variations:** Instead of pork, try shredded chicken, fish slices, or even thinly sliced beef. Cook chicken or fish during the last 15-20 minutes, or until cooked through.
*   **Vegetarian Options:** For a vegetarian version, omit the meat and use vegetable broth. Add sliced mushrooms (shiitake or oyster), corn kernels, or silken tofu for extra texture and protein.
*   **Other Eggs:** A common variation is "Preserved Egg and Salted Pork Congee" (皮蛋瘦肉粥), often served with salted duck egg alongside or instead of century egg, offering a different salty kick.
*   **Flavor Boosters:** Experiment with different broths. A dashi broth can give it a Japanese twist, while a stronger bone broth will add more richness. A pinch of five-spice powder can also enhance the pork flavor.
*   **Quick Congee:** For a faster preparation, use a pressure cooker. It can cook congee in about 20-30 minutes. Alternatively, using leftover cooked rice will also significantly reduce cooking time.

Congee with Century Egg is more than just a breakfast dish; it's a comforting embrace in a bowl, a testament to the simple yet profound flavors of traditional Chinese cooking. Whether you're seeking a hearty start to your day or a soothing meal, this timeless recipe offers a unique blend of textures and tastes that is both nourishing and deeply satisfying. Don't be intimidated by the century egg; its distinct character is what makes this dish truly special. Take the time to prepare it with care, and you’ll discover the enduring appeal of this beloved Chinese staple. Happy cooking!
